utility_details.utility_summary: Both runs produced high-quality product briefs that pass all 6 evaluation criteria. However, wi_skills is meaningfully stronger on J1 (target user specificity): it names a concrete persona with role and organizational context ('team leads, project managers, cross-functional operators in multi-seat B2B workspaces'), while wo_skills retains generic audience language throughout. On all other items (J2–J6), both outputs are comparably rigorous — both flag unvalidated assumptions, define anti-goals, scope an MVP, specify measurable metrics, and deliver conditional go/no-go recommendations backed by specific findings. The wi_skills run demonstrably applied the product-lens diagnostic framework (reading the SKILL.md, structuring output around the 7-question framework) and produced a slightly more opinionated, diagnostically structured brief. The wo_skills run, despite lacking the skill, independently arrived at a similarly critical and well-structured brief — suggesting the baseline agent is capable but slightly weaker on persona specificity. Overall, wi_skills produces higher quality output, primarily differentiated by the concrete user persona definition.
